title: Four Equidistant Points on a Grid

content.problemStatement:
The manhattan distance between two points P1(x1,y1)P_1(x_1, y_1)P1​(x1​,y1​) and P2(x2,y2)P_2(x_2, y_2)P2​(x2​,y2​) is given by d(P1,P2)=∣x2−x1∣+∣y2−y1∣d(P_1, P_2) = |x_2 - x_1| + |y_2 - y_1|d(P1​,P2​)=∣x2​−x1​∣+∣y2​−y1​∣.

In other words, manhattan distance is the minimum number of moves required to reach P2P_2P2​ from P1P_1P1​ if, in each move, you are allowed to travel one unit along the XXX-axis or one unit along the YYY-axis.

You are given an integer DDD. Find four points (P1,P2,P3,P4)(P_1, P_2, P_3, P_4)(P1​,P2​,P3​,P4​) with **integer** coordinates, such that:


The absolute value of both XXX and YYY coordinates of all points is at most 10910^9109.
The manhattan distance between **any pair** of points is DDD .
More formally, d(Pi,Pj)=Dd(P_i, P_j) = Dd(Pi​,Pj​)=D for all 1≤i<j≤41 \leq i < j \leq 41≤i<j≤4.
If such set of points do not exist, print `-1`. If there are multiple solutions, you may print any.

**Note:** It is guaranteed that whenever there exists a solution, there exists one in which all points have coordinates with absolute values not more than 10910^9109.


Input Format

The first line contains a single integer, DDD - as per the problem statement.


Output Format

If there is no solution, print in a single line the integer `-1`.
Otherwise print 444 lines. The ithi^{th}ith line, should contain two space separated integers, XiYiX_i Y_iXi​Yi​, the coordinates of the point PiP_iPi​, such that 0≤∣Xi∣,∣Yi∣≤1090 \leq |X_i|, |Y_i| \leq 10^90≤∣Xi​∣,∣Yi​∣≤109.
constraints: All coordinates in the output must satisfy |Xi|, |Yi| ≤ 10^9
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T – the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a single integer D (1 ≤ D ≤ 10^5).
content.outputFormat: For each test case, if no suitable set of four points exists, output a single line with -1. Otherwise output exactly four lines, each containing two space‑separated integers Xi Yi – the coordinates of the i‑th point. The points for different test cases are printed consecutively without extra blank lines.

sampleTestCases.explanation: First test case D=2 (even). h=1, points are (0,1),(1,0),(0,-1),(-1,0) – all pairwise Manhattan distances equal 2. Second test case D=1 (odd) – no solution, so -1.
D=4 is even, h=2. The four points satisfy the required distance of 4 between any pair.
Test cases: D=6 → h=3, D=8 → h=4, D=3 (odd) → -1.

solution.approach: For Manhattan distance the set of points {(0, h), (h, 0), (0, -h), (-h, 0)} where h = D/2 forms a regular simplex: every pair of points has distance |Δx|+|Δy| = h+h = D. This construction requires h to be integer, i.e., D must be even. If D is odd such a set cannot exist because the Manhattan distance between any two integer points has the same parity as the sum of the parity of their coordinate differences, which would be odd for some pair. Therefore: • If D is odd → output -1. • If D is even → let h = D/2 and output the four points above. The coordinates are within the required bounds because D ≤ 10^5 ⇒ h ≤ 5·10^4.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – constant work per test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few variables are used.
